speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number five. We're interviewing Isabella Leitner in New York City, New York, USA. The interviewer is Raquel Grunwald. The language of the interview is in English. And it's April 3rd, 1995.

speaker: interviewer
text: Isabella, we were talking about your experience in Auschwitz. Were there any acts of human kindness that you've witnessed at any point between the prisoners themselves?

speaker: interviewee
text: Many, many. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There were people who-- who couldn't bear their hunger and would steal from their own sister or brother, you know, to take a piece of bread because-- a tiny piece, because there was only a tiny piece and because they simply couldn't bear it. And I cannot judge them. On the other hand, I have experienced incredible kindness from my sister, who is responsible for our escape. And she had the kind of emotional strength that is pretty much unmatched by many people.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: Chica. She actually came to the-- to the revere in the second camp where I was on the verge of death already. I must've weighed about 40 pounds. I-- I know the way I looked because I looked at my sisters, and that was my mirror. And they were only eyes. There was no body, just bones and eyes. And-- my sister-- I was put in the revere, which my sister would not have allowed in Auschwitz because once you were put in the revere, you condemn yourself to the crematorium because from there, you were taken to the crematorium.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, the revere is a hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: The revere is their version of a hospital. The kind of hospital no one ever saw. But in-- in the second camp, there was no crematorium, so my sisters allowed me to be in the revere so that I would not have to stand in the zahlappell. That was the only saving grace of being in the revere. But it also meant that you were no longer a human being. I certainly was not. And I remember that I was-- I didn't have enough emotional and physical wherewithal to get up in the middle of the night and try to go out and defecate somewhere.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I defecated right there on the straw or on the ground. On the frozen ground that I was sleeping on. And stayed in it. I was that far gone. I could not have lasted for more than a few days.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was in that revere for some time. I don't know, a week or two or some such thing. And my three sisters came to visit me every night. They allowed them to come in to see their family. And I remember my sister coming in and bringing a piece of bread and saying, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it's-- 'I brought it for you. I am-- I am not hungry. I really am not hungry.' So that I should not have the guilt that goes with taking her piece of bread. And forced it on me and-- and-- and tried every which way to make me believe that she's not hungry. It was insane. But there was such kindness. She was not the only one. I also know that my sister, because she was working in Auschwitz in the unterkunft, and they were especially designated, those seven people. They had certain privileges of movement when there was [NON-ENGLISH]. When everybody had to go into the barracks, the only ones you could see outside were either the couples, or the Nazis, or the seven girls who were sent out to fetch water to wash some of that clothing. And they wore a white kerchief frequently, and that's how you could tell that they were the unterkunft people. And as soon as she heard that Mengele is in the camp selecting, she would grab the pitcher and ran out to get water to see if we were selected or not. But once she saw that it wasn't our barrack that was being selected, that we were safe in barrack 10 or 11 or 12, but it was number 13 that was being selected or so, then she came out with-- Then as she was walking with the pitcher to fetch the water from the so-called bathroom area, she would hand the pitcher over to a girl who was standing in a selected group for the crematorium. Because everybody else was locked into the-- into the barracks. But the people who were selected were taken out to be taken to the crematorium. On this occasion, they were doing that this way. Each time they did it differently.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say selected, you mean selected to die?

speaker: interviewee
text: Selected by Mengele, and they were lined up, and then the truck would come and put them on the truck, and they would go to the crematorium. And they knew that because they were the so-called Muselmanns. A Muselmann meant that you're ready for the crematorium. Why they called us the Muselmann, I never knew. Nobody knows to this day. I think. I never heard of that expression.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, Viktor Frankl talks about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know why they were called Muselmanns. But the ones who could no longer walk, but shuffle. And they were even thinner than I was, or the next guy. Who was evidently barely alive and was ready for the crematorium, that was-- that person was called a Muselmann. When she saw that group, and she knew that we were safe in another barrack, then she would take her pitcher and hand it over to the girl so that she would look like one because she still had the white kerchief, and she could look like one who is working in the unterkunft, therefore she's not with the group that's going to the crematorium. And she saved people innumerable times like that. There was such humanity. Or I remember, on one occasion in the second camp, where there was no crematorium to burn the dead, so we actually had to bury them. And the group was selected. The-- the-- the-- a number of were selected on this one occasion to bury a girl who died. And I was selected as one to carry the body, one out of four or five. And my sister Chica was selected as one who would dig the grave. The frozen earth. And she knew that I was much more afraid of a dead body than she was. That I was terrified. That I was really just a child in my heart, a terror-stricken child. And she had such empathy that she whispered to me, and she said, I could not change places with her and not carry the body. But she was able to pretend that she's walking along, and she whispered to me, and she said, I put my-- I'll put my hand under the body, and you put your hand on mine. So I didn't feel the cold body. Such exquisite humanity that you have experienced.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was my sister, but there are countless stories like that. Even though, when they put out the daily dead, as they died during the night, and they put them out in front of the barrack, so that the death commando could come and fetch the bodies and slap them onto this contraption to wheel them to the crematorium. When we found the dead bodies in the morning who were thrown out in front of the block, what do you think the first impulse is?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not tears or not sorrow for the person who's lying there dead. That person no longer feels anything. I on the other hand, feel the desperate cold. So what we would do is strip them of their clothing, and thereby you could-- you could acquire additional warmth-- additional warm clothing by having another layer. We did not strip them naked because then they would have noticed it. But we could peel off something that could help us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Or I could put my shoe on the dead body and get hers because hers was in better condition. You do understand what I'm trying to say? And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] right along with that kind of goodness of-- of giving your own bread and trying to convince you that you're not hungry, that went on at the same time, stripping the dead body. But even that was some kind of a-- or I-- you know, somebody might have taken off a piece of clothing and given it to somebody who had less than she had. So side by side, right along each other, there were acts of greatness, great humanity, and great deprivation of the human soul. Of such, you know, kind of a bestiality that would appear to be bestial. In truth, perhaps it was not. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: It's-- it's not possible to hand down the experience to the-- to the exact [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] shape of the way it really was because you simply-- you-- you just had to be there to know what it was like. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] You know, when we see films and all kinds of plays, we don't hear ourselves talk.

speaker: interviewee
text: Who speaks for us? The most important people in the drama are the prisoners. The most important actors in the drama are the prisoners, who were-- who were-- who can't-- they're the only ones who can pry open the inside of a person who was Hitler's prisoner.

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody else can speak for them, somehow. Try, they do. And-- and there are eloquent writings, introductions, and everything, but [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] who can lay the soul bare of the person who was the person.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me what happened after Auschwitz. I understand you went to another camp. How did that happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: After Auschwitz, we didn't-- we tried, in a sense, to stay in Auschwitz because getting away from Auschwitz meant that you had to be selected by Mengele. And we couldn't afford the selection because there were four of us, and he could select us in four different directions. One to another camp, one to the crematorium, one to yet another camp, one to remain in Auschwitz. So we couldn't afford. So we had to run as fast as we could. One night, we ran as he was selecting. And we ran out the back door. And he came after us, and he shot. And he missed us in the dark. He-- he missed all four of us. But we ran even at the possibility of being shot because it's better to be shot together than live alone. Because that was the only meaning to our existence, is to be together. To save what we can of what was-- of what is on the life side rather than in the crematorium. Well, we try to avoid the selections, therefore we stayed somewhat longer in Auschwitz than some of the others who had nobody to lose. It was-- you were alone, it was harder because you had no one to live for. At the same time, you didn't have to agonize of whether your sister is in the crematorium or not. So we stayed there until November. And by November, they were really dismantling Auschwitz and certainly dismantling Laager C, which was the Hungarian camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: November '44?

speaker: interviewee
text: November '44. And finally, we were selected to-- for a transport. But what the transport was was 1,000 girls who were selected to go out of the border, which said arbeit macht frei. I was never beyond that except on this one time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they took us out from that sign. You know? Arbeit macht. Work makes you free. And the ironies. [CHUCKLES] Work makes you free. And-- and they took us, indeed. They took us out, and they took us to the crematorium.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we stood there all night long, the thousand prisoners. And we were-- there was some sense of serenity about us and some kind-- we came to terms somehow with the whole thing because we were together, and the four of us will die together. And there is nothing we can do about this anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we stood in front of the-- and there was also the flames. And there was some kind of heat that made the November night a little less chilly while they were burning others, you know? And we stood there all night. A thousand girls?

speaker: interviewee
text: A thousand girls. And as they had done so many times before, by the morning, they changed orders, and they did not take us to the crematorium. They took us to-- on another transport. And they piled us into an ice cold train, one car of which was heated for the fat SS. They were fat. Some of them were so fat sometimes. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Germans? Were these Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were-- yeah, the Nazis. The SS who accompanied us. They were eating and drinking in the heated car. And we were in an ice cold train. And I don't know how long we rode, but we arrived somewhere. And when we arrived, they peeled us off the cattle car, and they put us in-- and-- and they took us to some kind of an-- a clearing, a green clearing in a forest. And they piled us onto open tractors. And then we saw the Nazis busily running about getting shovels. And that of course meant that we are going to dig our own graves. Well, this time, it wasn't that either. The shovels were for digging ditches, tank traps against the Russians, which we didn't know. But they took us to another part of the forest, where they had 10 what they called, telts. Wooden, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] umbrella-like structures. Paper thin wood. And each-- each one had hundred prisoners, and they piled us into those 10 structures. And every morning, they took us out the other end of the forest, marching through the town, where the traffic had to be stopped. And children were going to school, and people were going to work, and fresh bread from bakeries, and the smell of food of all kinds.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: In a town called, [NON-ENGLISH]. And today they say the length and breadth of Germany was filled these camps. And they saw us marching, these skeletons. And they say they never saw us. And the length and breadth of the country was filled either with death marchers or people like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: You marched through the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Every day, twice going and coming.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you saw the German people and the German children.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they didn't look at you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we were vermin. We were not human beings. They wouldn't waste a glance on Jews. And then they went home, and they were nice, loving to their cats and dogs. And today, you find me one single German who will admit that they-- that they did this to us. Find me one who ever harmed us, let alone they murdered us. You cannot find one single one who is guilty.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was not there. She was not there. He was somewhere else on the front. Here and there. Who did it? You know how much work goes into killing six million people? You know how much physical labor is involved? Who did it? But there isn't a single one who even 50 years later. They're all Ivan-- Ivan the Terrible was innocent, you see? There isn't a single SS who wasn't Ivan the Terrible.

speaker: interviewee
text: You don't have to identify him and have trials like that. He was. He was Ivan the Terrible, as every SS was. And then we stayed there in this forest until January 21st. And on January 21st, I'm trying to finish it up.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is this in Germany? Was [NON-ENGLISH] in Germany or Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Eastern Germany. It was Poland, then Germany. It was back and forth. But it was not far from Breslau.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and they-- on January 21, on Sunday, which was the day that we did not go out to do [INAUDIBLE], right? It was a bitter cold January. Eastern Germany is bitter cold. And it was January 21, and in the afternoon, suddenly we heard the oberscharfurer, you know, the top dog, saying, los, los, schnell, schnell immediately! We had to line up. And left the people who were in the revere, they didn't have time to pry them out and put them on stretchers and carry them. They couldn't walk because they had gangrene from digging so deep down that the water came. The [INAUDIBLE] tracks were dug that deep.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they left them there. And us, the nearly thousand prisoners, lined up, and immediately we were on our way, marching somewhere. And we marched until the night time. And where we-- they put us up in a-- an evacuated camp that was evacuated by another thousand prisoners who were continuing the death march. And then we woke up.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then the next day, Monday, we marched again. And Monday night, we were housed in an evacuated camp, which was a barn where they held the cows. And on top where they had the straw, we were housed there, and we buried ourselves in the straw.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your sisters and--

speaker: interviewee
text: The four of us. The four of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, with typical ingenuity, because who says the Jews are supposed to be so smart. They outsmarted us a thousand times a day, because evil is smarter than good. And they were evil. And they-- we hid in the straw. And then they boiled potatoes outside so that the smell of potato, boiled potato, would lure us out to the marchers, to the people who were already lined up.

speaker: interviewee
text: So if we hid anywhere in the straw, they had no time to pick us out. So they lured us out with the smell of the potato, and we fell for it. Some people didn't. In the end, 30 people in that area did survive. Those who hid in the straw.

speaker: interviewee
text: The four of us couldn't bear the smell of boiled potatoes. We came out, and we lined up. And by the time it reached us, there were no potatoes. But were back. We were the prisoners of Hitler again. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And those unconsumed potatoes that we never got cost us another sister. Because had we stayed there, there would've been four sisters alive today. But we couldn't bear the hunger, and we didn't know what it was all about. And we came out of our hiding place, and we were back in the line of marchers. And this was now Tuesday morning. And we marched until mid day. And then three girls in back of us escaped. And we always march five in a row. In the middle of the road, the snowy road, my sister Regina had one wooden shoe and one [INAUDIBLE]. And she was coughing so badly that no matter who gave her-- somebody would have to die because one would have to go one foot shoeless. And-- Regina-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we lined-- Yeah, it was Tuesday morning, and we were marching. The-- and-- and the three girls escape. And the five in the row. And on both sides are Nazis, and in the front and in the back. We reached a village called [NON-ENGLISH], a little German village. And my sister Chica, who was at this end of the line, noticed that there is no smoke coming on. There was no central heating. It was a little blacksmith's house, as later learned. And there was no smoke coming out, so nobody is there. Therefore, she targeted that as the house to escape to. And at the same time that she noticed this, the Nazis on the two sides went-- and the back-- went back to shoot two girls, I think, while one is digging the grave.

speaker: interviewee
text: So this gave my sister the opportunity to be unnoticed by the Germans who went back. And by this time, we were so badly off that we didn't talk to each other and didn't make plans to do everything together, but whoever can survive should. And she bolted and ran into the little house and up to the attic.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my little sister Regina also standing next to her saw her, and she ran. And I saw Regina, and I ran. And we were certain that Zippy, our oldest sister, ran into the next house, but we could not go look for it-- for her until-- until we are free.

speaker: interviewee
text: So Chica went upstairs to the attic, and she yelled down at one point, and she said, 'Hey, are you all here? I found a frozen cabbage, and it's delicious, and nobody's in the house.' And we yelled up and said, 'No. There are only the two of us here. Zippy is probably in another house. ' And suddenly we heard, after this-- either prior or after this exchange, we heard the crackling of the snow, and the top dog came with his big German Shepard. He was coming back from the killing. And he was cursing, 'The goddamn Jews! How dare they to-- they escape, and we have to go and bury them? How dare they?' And he was talking to himself. And all this time, while he's talking to himself, Regina and I are hiding in a little dog house that was emptied.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because the Germans escaped from the Russians, and they took along their dog, of course. And the house was empty. And that little dog house. First, we were crouching in the dog house. Then we climbed out. And that's when we heard the German cursing to himself. And he passed us by. He didn't notice us. And the dog evidently, didn't smell us because the wind was so great it blew our smell another way, probably. And finally, he was gone. And then-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and then we climbed out of the little dog house. And we went up to the attic.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. You know what? We're going to finish this on our next tape. And thank you very much up to now.
